questions.question:
A set of Boolean connectives is functionally complete if all Boolean functions can be synthesized using those. Which of the following sets of connectives is NOT functionally complete?

A. EX-NOR
B. Implication, negation
C. OR, negation
D. NAND
questions.to_find: Which set of Boolean connectives is NOT functionally complete

understand.plain: A functionally complete set of connectives can express every possible Boolean function. We need to find which of the given sets cannot do this. EX-NOR (XNOR) alone cannot generate all Boolean functions because using only XNOR you can only produce functions that are affine (linear mod 2 plus a constant) — NOT is not expressible with XNOR alone.

keywords.explain: A set of logical operators is functionally complete if every Boolean function can be expressed using only those operators.
keywords.example: {NAND} alone is functionally complete: NOT a = NAND(a,a), AND a,b = NAND(NAND(a,b),NAND(a,b))

keywords.explain: XNOR(a,b) = 1 if a and b have the same value; it equals NOT(a XOR b).

understand.visual_alt: Four candidate sets: {XNOR}, {->, NOT}, {OR, NOT}, {NAND}. Only XNOR alone is not functionally complete.

given.aim: Determine which set of connectives cannot synthesize all Boolean functions.

terms.meaning: XNOR gate: output 1 when both inputs are equal
terms.connects: Need to check if XNOR alone can produce NOT and AND

terms.meaning: a -> b = NOT(a) OR b; combined with NOT gives a complete basis
terms.example: NOT via NOT(a); OR via NOT(a) -> b after De Morgan
terms.connects: Implication+NOT is equivalent to {OR,NOT} which is complete

terms.meaning: NOT of AND; the Sheffer stroke — by itself functionally complete
terms.connects: NAND alone can express every Boolean function

given.plan: Check each option. A set is complete if it can express NOT and AND (or NOT and OR). Options B, C, D are all known to be functionally complete. For XNOR alone: XNOR(a,a)=1 (constant 1), but we cannot get NOT(a) without a constant 0, and with only XNOR we cannot produce an odd-parity function like NOT(a). Therefore XNOR alone is NOT functionally complete.

steps.title: Check options B, C, D for completeness
steps.apply: B: {->, NOT} — implication with negation covers {OR, NOT} which is complete. C: {OR, NOT} — De Morgan: NOT(NOT(a) OR NOT(b)) = AND(a,b), so AND is derivable; this is complete. D: {NAND} — Sheffer stroke, classically functionally complete on its own.
steps.note: All three of B, C, D are functionally complete.

steps.title: Check option A: XNOR alone
steps.formula_raw: XNOR(a,b) = NOT(a XOR b) = a XNOR b
steps.apply: With only XNOR: XNOR(a,a) = 1 (gives constant 1). XNOR(1,a) = NOT(a) only if we have constant 1, which we just derived. So NOT(a) = XNOR(a, XNOR(a,a)). Can we get AND? AND(a,b) = NOT(NOT(a) OR NOT(b)). NOT(a) OR NOT(b) = NOT(a XOR b XOR 1) ... Actually, the set of functions expressible by XNOR is closed under affine (linear) Boolean functions (XOR-closure). AND is NOT a linear function, so AND cannot be expressed using XNOR alone (even with constants). Therefore XNOR alone is NOT functionally complete.
steps.note: The key insight: XNOR-closed functions are all affine Boolean functions. Non-linear functions like AND cannot be generated.

solution.result: Option A (EX-NOR alone) is NOT functionally complete because it cannot express non-linear functions like AND.

questions.formula_note: A set of connectives is functionally complete iff it is not a subset of any of the five Post classes (T0, T1, S, M, L). XNOR alone belongs to the class of linear (affine) functions and cannot generate AND.

questions.question:
The decimal value 0.5 in IEEE single precision floating point representation has

A. fraction bits of 000...0 and exponent value of 0
B. fraction bits of 000...0 and exponent value of -1
C. fraction bits of 100...0 and exponent value of 0
D. no exact representation
questions.answer: B. fraction bits of 000...0 and exponent value of -1
questions.to_find: IEEE 754 single-precision representation of the decimal value 0.5

understand.plain: We need to convert 0.5 into IEEE 754 single-precision floating point format (1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits with bias 127, 23 fraction bits). 0.5 = 1.0 × 2^(-1), so the stored fraction is all zeros and the unbiased exponent is -1.

keywords.explain: 32-bit float: 1 sign bit, 8 biased-exponent bits (bias=127), 23 fraction (mantissa) bits.
The stored exponent = actual exponent + 127 (bias). This allows representation of both positive and negative exponents.
keywords.example: Actual exponent -1 is stored as -1+127=126 = 01111110 in binary

given.aim: Convert 0.5 to IEEE 754 single-precision and identify fraction bits and exponent value.

terms.meaning: The bits stored in the 23-bit mantissa field (the part after the implicit leading 1)
terms.example: 1.0 × 2^(-1) has fractional part .0, so all 23 fraction bits are 0

terms.meaning: The true (unbiased) exponent, not the stored biased value
terms.example: Stored exponent 126 = 01111110 corresponds to true exponent 126-127 = -1
terms.connects: The question asks for exponent value = -1

given.plan: Write 0.5 in normalized binary form (1.0 × 2^(-1)). The fraction bits are all 0. The exponent value (unbiased) is -1. Match with the options.

steps.title: Normalize 0.5 in binary
steps.apply: 0.5 in binary is 0.1. In normalized IEEE 754 form, we write it as 1.0 × 2^(-1). The leading 1 is implicit (not stored), and the fractional part after the decimal point is .0 — all 23 fraction bits are 0.
steps.note: IEEE 754 always normalizes so the significand is 1.xxx, the leading 1 is implicit.

steps.title: Identify fraction bits and exponent value
steps.apply: Fraction bits = 000...0 (23 zeros, since fractional part of 1.0 is 0). Exponent value (actual/unbiased) = -1. Stored exponent = 126 = 0111 1110. Full 32-bit pattern: 0 01111110 00000000000000000000000 = 0x3F000000.
steps.note: The question asks for 'exponent value' which means the unbiased exponent = -1, not the stored value 126.

solution.result: Fraction bits are 000...0 and exponent value is -1. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: IEEE 754 single-precision: value = (-1)^S × 1.M × 2^(E-127), where S is sign, E is stored 8-bit exponent, M is 23-bit mantissa.

questions.question:
The value of a float type variable is represented using the single-precision floating point format of IEEE 754 standard that uses 1 bit for sign, 8 bits for biased exponent and 23 bits for the mantissa. A float type variable X is assigned the decimal value of -14.25. The representation of X in hexadecimal notation is

A. 0xC1640000
B. 0x416C0000
C. 0xC16C0000
D. 0x414C0000

understand.plain: We convert -14.25 to IEEE 754 32-bit format. The number is negative (sign=1), we find the binary representation of 14.25, normalize it, compute the biased exponent, extract the 23-bit mantissa, then read off the 32-bit hex value.

keywords.explain: Stored exponent = actual exponent + 127. For exponent 3, stored = 130 = 10000010.
The 23 bits stored after the implicit leading 1 in the normalized form 1.xxx × 2^e.
keywords.example: 1.11001 × 2^3 has mantissa bits 11001 followed by 18 zeros

understand.visual_alt: Sign=1 (negative), Exponent=130=10000010, Mantissa=11001000000000000000000. Combined 32-bit value = C1640000 hex.

given.aim: Find the 32-bit IEEE 754 single-precision hex representation of -14.25.

terms.meaning: Negative decimal number to be converted to IEEE 754

given.plan: Convert 14.25 to binary (1110.01), normalize to 1.11001 × 2^3, compute biased exponent = 130 = 10000010, extract 23-bit mantissa = 11001 followed by 18 zeros. Set sign=1. Combine all bits and convert to hex.

steps.title: Convert 14.25 to binary and normalize
steps.apply: 14 in binary = 1110. 0.25 in binary = 0.01. So 14.25 = 1110.01. Normalize: move binary point 3 places left -> 1.11001 × 2^3. Exponent = 3.
steps.note: The fractional part 0.25 = 1/4 = 2^(-2), giving the bit pattern 0.01.

solution.result: The hexadecimal representation of -14.25 in IEEE 754 single precision is 0xC1640000. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: IEEE 754 single precision: sign(1) + biased exponent(8, bias=127) + mantissa(23). Value = (-1)^S × 1.M × 2^(E-127).

questions.question:
Consider the following representation of a number in IEEE 754 single-precision floating point format with a bias of 127.

S : 0
E : 10000000
F : 11111111111111111111111

Here S, E and F denote the sign, exponent, and fraction components of the floating point representation.

The decimal value corresponding to the above representation (rounded to 2 decimal places) is __
questions.to_find: The decimal value of the given IEEE 754 single-precision bit pattern, rounded to 2 decimal places

understand.plain: We are given the bit fields of an IEEE 754 single-precision number: sign = 0 (positive), stored exponent E = 10000000 (binary) = 128, and fraction F = 23 ones. We compute: actual exponent = 128 - 127 = 1, significand = 1.11111111111111111111111 in binary = 2 - 2^(-23). The value = 2 × (2 - 2^(-23)) which is just under 4.00.

keywords.explain: The 8-bit biased exponent field. Actual exponent = E - bias = E - 127.
The 23-bit field after the implicit leading 1 in the significand 1.F.

given.aim: Compute the decimal value of the IEEE 754 bit pattern S=0, E=10000000, F=all 1s.

terms.connects: Significand is just slightly less than 2

given.plan: Apply the formula: value = (-1)^S × 1.F × 2^(E-127). Compute 1.F = 1 + (1 - 2^(-23)) = 2 - 2^(-23). Multiply by 2^1 = 2. Round to 2 decimal places.

steps.title: Compute actual exponent and significand
steps.note: All 23 fraction bits being 1 gives the largest possible significand just below 2.

steps.title: Compute the final value
steps.note: 2^(-22) is extremely small (~2.38 × 10^(-7)), so to 2 decimal places the result is exactly 4.00.

solution.result: The decimal value is 4 - 2^(-22) ≈ 3.9999998, which rounds to 4.00.

questions.formula_note: IEEE 754 value formula: (-1)^S × 1.F × 2^(E-bias). For single precision, bias = 127.

questions.question:
The format of a single-precision floating point number as per the IEEE 754 standard is as follows:

| Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
| (1 bit) | (8 bits) | (23 bits) |

Which one of the following choices is correct with respect to the smallest normalized positive number represented using the standard?

A.  Sign=0, Exponent=00000001, Mantissa=00000000000000000000000
B.  Sign=0, Exponent=11111110, Mantissa=11111111111111111111111
C.  Sign=0, Exponent=11111111, Mantissa=00000000000000000000000
D.  Sign=0, Exponent=00000001, Mantissa=11111111111111111111111
questions.to_find: The IEEE 754 single-precision bit pattern corresponding to the smallest normalized positive number

understand.plain: The smallest normalized positive number in IEEE 754 single precision has the smallest possible biased exponent (not zero, since E=0 is reserved for denormals/zero) and the smallest mantissa. The smallest valid biased exponent is 1 (E=00000001), giving actual exponent = 1-127 = -126. The smallest mantissa is all zeros, giving significand = 1.0. So the smallest normalized positive number is 1.0 × 2^(-126).

keywords.explain: A floating-point number with a biased exponent E in range 1 to 254 (not 0 or 255). The significand is implicitly 1.xxx.
keywords.example: E=00000001 is the smallest valid normalized exponent; E=00000000 means denormalized or zero

keywords.explain: Achieved with minimum exponent (E=1, actual=-126) and minimum mantissa (all 0s, significand=1.0).

understand.visual_alt: Smallest normalized positive: Sign=0, Exponent=00000001 (stored=1, actual=-126), Mantissa=00000000000000000000000.

given.aim: Identify the bit pattern for the smallest normalized positive IEEE 754 single-precision number.

terms.meaning: E = 00000001 = 1; E=0 is reserved for denormals/zero, so 1 is the minimum for normalized
terms.connects: Gives the smallest power of 2 scale factor for normalized numbers

terms.meaning: All 23 mantissa bits = 0, giving significand 1.0 (implicit leading 1 + 0 fraction)
Reserved for infinity (mantissa=0) and NaN (mantissa != 0), not a normalized number
terms.connects: Option C is infinity, not the smallest normalized positive

given.plan: The smallest normalized positive number = smallest valid exponent (E=1, not 0) combined with smallest mantissa (all zeros). E=0 is reserved for denormalized; E=255 is reserved for inf/NaN. So answer is A.

steps.title: Identify valid exponent range for normalized numbers
steps.formula_raw: E in {1, 2, ..., 254} for normalized; E=0 -> denormal/zero; E=255 -> inf/NaN
steps.apply: Option A: E=00000001=1 (valid, actual=-126). Option B: E=11111110=254 (actual=127, this is actually the LARGEST exponent for normalized). Option C: E=11111111=255 (reserved for infinity/NaN, not normalized). Option D: E=00000001=1 but mantissa=all 1s (larger than option A).
steps.note: E=0 is NOT used for normalized numbers; smallest valid E for normalized = 1.

steps.title: Select the pattern with smallest value
steps.apply: Option A: 1.0 × 2^(-126) — smallest possible (exponent -126, mantissa 0). Option D: (2-2^(-23)) × 2^(-126) — same exponent but larger mantissa. Option B: (2-2^(-23)) × 2^127 — huge positive. Option C: infinity. Therefore A is the smallest normalized positive number.
steps.note: Among options with E=1 (A and D), A has all-zero mantissa so it is smaller.

solution.result: The smallest normalized positive IEEE 754 single-precision number is Sign=0, Exponent=00000001, Mantissa=000...0. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: IEEE 754 single precision normalized range: exponent E in [1,254], value = 1.M × 2^(E-127). Smallest normalized positive = 1.0 × 2^(-126) with E=1, M=0.
